answer:
**No verbatim definition of *cidātma* ('consciousness-self') appears in the Falsafa corpus edition of *Vr̥haspatitattva* (a Medieval Kawi prose work attributed to Bṛhaspati).**[^1]

The closest discussion occurs in the explanation of how the pure *Śivatattva* (conscious, crystal-like) becomes bound in *māyā*, taking on *mala* (unconscious impurity). Its all-knowing, all-doing power is veiled, at which point it is termed *ātmā*—explicitly glossed as **'dazed consciousness'** (*cetana leṅleṅleṅ*, i.e., consciousness rendered insentient or forgetful). This bound *ātmā* forgets its nature, contracts (*saṃkoca*), and enters *saṃsāra* amid the crowding of *māyātattva* (like bees in a honeycomb).[^2][^3]

> It is then called *ātmā* (soul), which means ‘dazed consciousness.’ These *ātmatattvas* are many, and so *māyātattva* is crowded, like a honeycomb, layered and stacked. *Māyā* is like the honeycomb, and the *ātmā* is like the young bees. They are face-down, which means they are bent over; the *ātmā* looks only downward, unaware of the *tattva* above it.[^2]

This aligns with Śaiva concepts of *cidātman* as the soul (*pāśupuruṣa* or *bound ātman*) whose consciousness (*cit*) is obscured by *mala*. Pure *cetana* (*Śivatattva*) precedes it (verses 6–10), while the bound state involves *pradhāna*-induced forgetting.[^4]

[^1]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, ch.1, full translation (no matches for "cidātma[n]", "cid-ātma[n]", or "consciousness-self"; searches exhausted all variants). [Chapter](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, ch.1, [p-643885]. [Paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).

[^3]: Cf. [p-ff7b52]–[p-c31df3] on *cetana* (pure consciousness: eternally aware, unobscured) vs. *ātmā*. [Passage](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-ff7b52)–[p-c31df3]).

[^4]: Later: "That is what you called ‘conscious’. It is called *cetana-saṃsāra*" ([p-7171d3]); *pradhāna* causes sleep-like forgetting ([p-695576]). [Paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-7171d3).
